pair_convention.spot_explanation: EUR/AUD at 1.6736 means 1 EUR buys 1.6736 AUD. A rising EUR/AUD means EUR is strengthening against AUD — each EUR buys more AUD. A falling EUR/AUD means EUR is weakening against AUD — each EUR buys fewer AUD. The pair is currently at the 25th percentile of its 52-week range (1.6130–1.8556), reflecting significant EUR weakness over the past 6–12 months driven by the widening ECB-RBA rate differential.

executive_summary: EUR/AUD trades at 1.6736, representing a sharp decline of approximately 5.5% over six months and 5.5% over the past year, as the RBA's unprecedented hawkish divergence from the ECB has driven the pair into a well-defined downtrend. The pair sits at the 25th percentile of its 52-week range (high 1.8556, low 1.6130), having rallied approximately 2.6% off the March 11 low of 1.6130 but remaining firmly below all major moving averages (50d at 1.657, 100d at 1.6924, 200d at 1.7346). The dominant bilateral narrative is a widening ECB-RBA rate differential — now -210bps and set to expand further as the RBA hikes toward 4.85% while the ECB's path remains uncertain between holding and modest tightening. This rate divergence has been amplified by the Iran-US war, which creates asymmetric bilateral effects: the energy shock is unambiguously EUR-negative (eurozone is a net energy importer facing a terms-of-trade deterioration) while its impact on AUD is mixed (net commodity exporter benefiting from coal/LNG surge offsetting oil import costs). Our tactical stance is Bearish EUR/AUD (conviction 3/5, confidence 63%), driven by the carry differential, rate path divergence, and technical momentum. Our strategic stance is also Bearish EUR/AUD (conviction 4/5, confidence 68%), as the structural forces — widening rate differentials, improving Australian terms of trade, German fiscal impulse still months from materialising — all point to further downside toward 1.60-1.63 over 1-6 months.

market_context.valuation_assessment: The EUR dossier notes EUR is approximately 4% undervalued versus AUD on a PPP basis, with an implied fair value around 1.58 (EUR/AUD terms). The AUD dossier notes AUD is approximately 4.2% undervalued versus EUR, implying fair value around AUD/EUR 0.623 or EUR/AUD ~1.606. These PPP estimates converge on a fair value zone of 1.58-1.61, suggesting the current spot of 1.6736 carries approximately 4-6% overvaluation relative to PPP — consistent with EUR/AUD being above equilibrium. The BIS REER for EUR at 103.85 (52nd percentile) versus AUD at 100.3 (25th percentile) reinforces AUD's relative cheapness in real trade-weighted terms.

EUR positioning is more vulnerable to liquidation, creating an asymmetric unwind risk that is modestly EUR/AUD bearish.

market_context.recent_regime: EUR/AUD has been in a persistent downtrend since the April 2025 tariff-shock spike to 1.8556 (52-week high). The pair declined roughly 1,400 pips (7.5%) from that peak through mid-May, then consolidated in a broad 1.73-1.81 range from June through October 2025. The decisive break lower came in Q4 2025 and accelerated in Q1 2026 as the RBA hiked twice (February and March) while the ECB held. The March 2026 low of 1.6130 coincided with peak AUD strength following the RBA's March 17 hike. The subsequent bounce to 1.6736 is a corrective retracement within the larger downtrend, driven by the Iran war's risk-off impulse temporarily benefiting EUR over AUD.

Over 6 months, carry accumulates to approximately +105 pips, which is more meaningful relative to the expected directional move.

bilateral_carry_analysis.carry_assessment: The ECB deposit rate at 2.00% versus the RBA cash rate at 4.10% creates a -210bps annualised carry cost for long EUR/AUD positions. This is the widest ECB-RBA differential in modern history, and it is set to widen further: OIS markets price the RBA terminal rate at approximately 4.85% by year-end, while the ECB path ranges from 2.00% (hold) to 2.75% (3 hikes). In the base case (RBA at 4.60%, ECB at 2.25%), the differential expands to -235bps. In the widest-divergence scenario (RBA 4.85%, ECB 2.00%), it reaches -285bps. The carry-to-vol ratio for long EUR/AUD is approximately -0.18 (210bps / ~12% vol), making it an unattractive hold. The carry regime is assessed as 'partially working' — the pair has declined broadly in line with the carry direction, though risk-off episodes can temporarily override carry as demonstrated by the recent bounce from 1.6130.

factors.weight_adjustment_reason: Increased from 0.24 default by +0.03 at both horizons due to extreme rate differential (200bps) and both central banks in active divergent policy modes — this is the dominant pair driver.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The ECB-RBA rate differential at -210bps is the most powerful single driver of EUR/AUD. The RBA at 4.10% is the highest rate in G10, actively hiking with a 70% probability of +25bps on May 5, while the ECB at 2.00% has pivoted hawkish but faces genuine uncertainty about follow-through (40-50% April hike probability). The divergence is directionally clear: the RBA is data-confirmed hawkish (back-to-back hikes, 5-4 vote on timing not direction, AG Kent suggesting neutral rate reassessment higher), while the ECB's hawkish signals are conditional on energy-driven inflation that may prove transitory. The AU-Bund 10Y spread at +193bps creates a powerful yield-seeking flow incentive favouring AUD over EUR-denominated fixed income. Even if the ECB delivers one hike to 2.25% in June, the differential widens if the RBA simultaneously hikes to 4.35% in May and 4.60% in June. The only tactical scenario where EUR/AUD rises on rates is a surprise ECB hike combined with an RBA pause — a low-probability outcome (~10%).

The edge is in the skew: if the RBA delivers more than priced (terminal above 4.85%) while the ECB underwhelms (holds through June), the differential widens beyond market expectations, providing additional EUR/AUD downside.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 1-6 months, the rate divergence story intensifies. The RBA is projected to reach 4.85% by August (Westpac), creating a policy rate spread of approximately +285bps over the ECB if the ECB holds, or +210-235bps even if the ECB hikes twice. This represents the widest ECB-RBA spread in the history of the euro and creates a structural carry flow into AUD-denominated assets. The AU-Bund 10Y spread at +198bps is at multi-decade wides, attracting real-money allocators including Japanese institutions (who purchased ¥213.6bn of AGBs in June 2025). The ECB's hawkish pivot, while real, is constrained by peripheral spread concerns (BTP-Bund at 95bp), whereas the RBA faces no such constraint on its fiscal credibility. The strategic rate picture is unambiguously EUR/AUD bearish — the only scenario for rate-driven pair appreciation is a sudden RBA reversal, which requires GDP below 1.5% or unemployment above 5.0%, neither of which is remotely priced.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The inflation-growth differential is starkly different between the two economies and points unambiguously to EUR/AUD downside. Australia's GDP at 2.6% YoY is more than double the eurozone's 1.2%, and the gap is widening as the energy shock disproportionately damages the eurozone (net energy importer) while Australia benefits from coal/LNG export revenue surges. The eurozone Composite PMI at 50.5 is teetering on contraction while the ZEW collapsed 58.8 points; Australia's labour market added 48,900 jobs versus 20,300 expected, with unemployment holding at 4.3%. On inflation, both economies face upward pressure from the oil shock, but the market reaction function differs: higher Australian inflation reinforces RBA hiking (AUD-positive through rates), while higher eurozone inflation creates a stagflation trap (EUR-negative through growth damage). The March HICP flash (Germany 2.8%, Spain 3.3%) will likely show headline spiking toward 2.5-3.0%, which validates the ECB's hawkish pivot but simultaneously destroys the growth outlook — a toxic combination for EUR.

The market has partially priced this but not fully — a hot print above 4.5% would force additional tightening expectations.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, Australia's growth advantage is structural: the economy has more capacity to absorb rate hikes (GDP at 2.6% with fiscal headroom) than the eurozone (GDP at 0.9% with peripheral fiscal constraints). The German fiscal impulse (€500bn infrastructure fund) is the main counterargument — it could add 0.3-0.5pp to eurozone GDP by 2027 — but the spending pipeline takes 6-12 months to materialise and is currently being overwhelmed by the energy shock. Australia's energy export revenue surge (thermal coal +40%, LNG +85%) will materially improve Q2 trade data, strengthening the fundamental case for AUD relative to EUR. The risk to this view is if China's growth disappoints (sub-4% GDP), which would undermine AUD's commodity channel.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: In acute risk-off episodes, EUR/AUD tends to rise because AUD has a higher beta to risk sentiment than EUR. AUD is the quintessential risk-on currency (high beta to equities, commodities, China) while EUR occupies a middle ground — not a safe haven but not as risk-sensitive as AUD. With VIX at 30.6, S&P 500 down 7% YTD, and the April 6 Iran deadline creating immediate binary risk, the risk-off impulse tactically supports EUR/AUD. The pair's recent bounce from 1.6130 to 1.6736 was driven partly by this risk-off dynamic. However, the pair's risk beta is moderate — EUR/AUD is not AUD/JPY — because EUR itself weakens in severe risk-off episodes (safe-haven USD demand). The net effect is that risk-off provides a modest tailwind for EUR/AUD, partially offsetting the carry and rate differential headwinds.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The energy shock creates a stark bilateral asymmetry that is the single clearest directional signal for EUR/AUD. Applying the commodity direction rules: Oil ↑ → AUD mildly positive (+), EUR negative (−). TTF gas ↑ → EUR negative (−), AUD neutral. LNG ↑ → AUD positive (+). Iron ore stable → AUD neutral. Coal ↑ → AUD positive (+). The netting is clear: for EUR/AUD, the commodity environment is unambiguously pair-negative. The eurozone is a net energy importer facing a potential current account surplus-to-deficit swing (from +1.6% GDP to potentially -1% GDP at sustained $110 oil). Australia is a net commodity exporter with coal/LNG revenues surging — thermal coal +40%, LNG +85%. The EU's bilateral trade with Australia totals €49.4 billion a year for goods and €38.1 billion for services; the EU exports €37 billion and imports €10.7 billion, resulting in a structural EU trade surplus of €28 billion — meaning EU entities are natural sellers of AUD, but the magnitude is modest relative to the macro commodity channel.

tactical.what_is_priced: The market has partially priced the energy asymmetry through EUR/AUD's 5.5% decline over 6 months. However, the Q2 trade data — which will show the full extent of Australia's energy export revenue surge and the eurozone's trade balance deterioration — is not yet available and will provide further confirmation.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The strategic terms-of-trade picture reinforces the bearish EUR/AUD view. Australia's net commodity export position means sustained elevated energy prices are net positive for the trade balance, while the eurozone's net import dependence (58% of total energy) creates a persistent drag. The IMF estimates each $10/bbl oil increase produces a net A$3-7B annual benefit for Australia. The EU-Australia FTA concluded on March 24, 2026, which will increase bilateral trade by approximately one-third and generate EUR-selling/AUD-buying flows as European investment into Australia expands — with the EU Commission projecting a potential increase of EU investment flows of over 87% into Australia. The EU's structural goods trade surplus with Australia (€28B) means structural AUD selling by EU entities, but this is dominated by the macro commodity channel that favours AUD.

strategic.what_is_priced: The EU-Australia FTA is not yet priced in the pair as the implementation timeline is unclear and flows will materialise over years. The near-term Australian energy export revenue surge is also not priced in data — it represents an identifiable mispricing.

factors.horizon_split: Both bearish, with tactical confidence higher (70 vs 65) because the near-term commodity price impact is mechanically EUR/AUD negative, while strategically the FTA and energy revenue flows are delayed but structurally supportive.

net_commodity_exposure.base_commodity_profile: EUR is net commodity NEGATIVE — the eurozone is a net energy importer with 58% import dependence. Oil ↑ and gas ↑ are unambiguously EUR-negative through terms-of-trade deterioration and current account swing from +1.6% GDP to potentially -1% GDP at sustained $110 oil.
net_commodity_exposure.quote_commodity_profile: AUD is net commodity POSITIVE — Australia is a net commodity exporter. Thermal coal (+40%) and LNG (+85%) surges directly boost export revenue. Oil ↑ is mildly positive for AUD (net benefit after oil import costs). Iron ore (~$106/T) is stable to positive. The net commodity basket is strongly AUD-positive in the current energy shock environment.
net_commodity_exposure.net_effect_calculation: Step 1 — Oil ($111-113/bbl): Oil ↑ → EUR negative (terms-of-trade deterioration as importer), AUD mildly positive (net commodity exporter, oil import cost partially offset by energy export revenues). Net pair effect: Bearish EUR/AUD. Step 2 — TTF Gas (€55/MWh): Gas ↑ → EUR strongly negative (direct energy cost to EZ economy), AUD neutral (Australia exports LNG but domestic gas exposure limited). Net pair effect: Bearish EUR/AUD. Step 3 — LNG (JKM $20.40/MMBtu, +85%): LNG ↑ → AUD positive (major LNG export revenues), EUR neutral/slightly negative. Net pair effect: Bearish EUR/AUD. Step 4 — Thermal Coal ($135-149/T, +40%): Coal ↑ → AUD positive (major thermal coal exporter), EUR mildly negative (import cost). Net pair effect: Bearish EUR/AUD. Step 5 — Iron Ore (~$106/T): Stable → AUD broadly neutral. Net pair effect: Neutral. Total netting: All four active commodity factors are Bearish EUR/AUD. The commodity environment is unambiguously EUR/AUD negative in the current energy shock regime.
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_up_10pct: Oil +10% (~$122/bbl) → EUR/AUD falls further. EZ current account deteriorates by approximately 0.3pp GDP, ECB faces deeper stagflation trap, AUD gains additional +A$3-7B annual trade benefit. Net effect: additional 50-80 pip EUR/AUD downside on a sustained basis.
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_down_10pct: Oil -10% (~$100/bbl) → EUR/AUD rises modestly. EZ energy import bill reduces, current account stabilises, stagflation risk moderates. AUD loses some commodity premium but coal/LNG support persists. The rate differential dominates at lower oil — net effect: modest 30-50 pip EUR/AUD upside, partially offset by carry reassertion.
net_commodity_exposure.dominant_commodity_driver: TTF natural gas is the dominant commodity driver for EUR/AUD given its direct impact on the eurozone's energy cost base and current account — the eurozone has no comparable offsetting energy export. LNG/coal provide the AUD-positive mirror. The TTF-LNG spread is the most important commodity pair signal to monitor.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The RBA's communication is decisively hawkish with high credibility: Governor Bullock confirmed further tightening is needed, AG Kent suggested neutral rate reassessment higher, and the March 17 minutes described the oil shock as necessitating a more restrictive stance. The ECB's communication is hawkish but ambiguous: Lagarde's 'measured adjustment' leaves open whether April sees action, while Governing Council dispersion (Nagel hawkish, Villeroy cautious) introduces uncertainty. The credibility differential is clear — the RBA is delivering on guidance (back-to-back hikes), while the ECB has reversed direction multiple times (8 cuts then halt, now potential tightening). For EUR/AUD, RBA credibility means the carry differential is reliable, while ECB uncertainty means the opposite leg could surprise in either direction.

tactical.what_is_priced: The RBA's guidance is well-priced; the ECB's is partially priced. tactical.score_interpretation: A net weighted score of -0.369 is clearly in bearish territory for EUR/AUD. Six factors are bearish EUR/AUD, two are bullish (risk appetite and event risk — both temporary), and two are neutral. The bearish factors are concentrated in the highest-weighted areas (Policy & Rates at 0.27, Inflation & Growth at 0.18), giving the directional signal strong structural backing. A score of -0.369 maps to conviction 3/5 — a clear directional view with the primary risk being the short-term risk-off impulse from the April 6 Iran deadline, which could temporarily push the pair higher before the bearish structural forces reassert.
tactical.rationale: The tactical EUR/AUD bearish case is built on three reinforcing pillars that collectively represent 52% of the tactical weighting: Policy & Rates (0.27, Bearish on the -210bps ECB-RBA differential widening toward -285bps), Inflation & Growth (0.18, Bearish on Australia's 2.6% GDP vs eurozone's 1.2% and the asymmetric energy shock impact), and Guidance & Credibility (0.07, Bearish on RBA's clear hawkish path versus ECB's uncertain direction). These factors interact powerfully: the RBA's credibility reinforces the carry signal, which attracts flows that validate the rate differential, creating a virtuous cycle for AUD versus EUR. The interaction between the standalone dossier views is instructive: both EUR and AUD dossiers are tactically bearish against USD, but for EUR/AUD the relative analysis reveals that EUR's weakness sources (stagflation, positioning vulnerability, terms-of-trade deterioration) are more fundamental than AUD's weakness sources (risk-off beta, positioning unwind) — suggesting EUR underperforms AUD even in a jointly bearish environment. The two bullish factors (Risk Appetite +0.048, Event Risk +0.033) are genuine near-term headwinds — the April 6 deadline could produce a 200+ pip spike in EUR/AUD on escalation — but they are temporary and carry-negative, meaning time works against holding a long EUR/AUD position. External Balance & Terms of Trade (Factor 5, 0.06) adds incremental bearish weight as the commodity environment is unambiguously EUR/AUD negative with oil at $111-113 and LNG +85%. Market Structure & Volatility (Factor 9, 0.05) confirms the bearish bias through the descending channel, below key MAs, and RSI near 40. The path of least resistance is lower, toward 1.6350-1.6500, with the April 6 deadline creating the primary risk of a disruptive squeeze higher before the trend reasserts. The net of all ten factors reflects a pair where the structural bearish drivers are clear and well-supported, but near-term risk-off events introduce meaningful short-term volatility risk.

strategic.score_interpretation: A net weighted score of -0.604 is strongly bearish — eight of ten factors are bearish, two are neutral, and none are bullish. This represents a rare degree of directional alignment across the framework for EUR/AUD. A score of -0.604 maps to conviction 4/5 — high conviction driven by the structural convergence of rate differentials, external balance dynamics, credibility, and technical momentum all pointing in the same direction.
strategic.rationale: The strategic EUR/AUD bearish thesis rests on four mutually reinforcing structural pillars. First, the rate differential is the dominant driver: the RBA at a projected terminal of 4.85% versus the ECB at 2.00-2.75% creates the widest ECB-RBA spread in history, attracting systematic carry flows and real-money allocation shifts into AUD-denominated assets. The AU-Bund 10Y spread at +198bps and heading toward +220-250bps creates a persistent yield-seeking flow incentive. Second, the external balance asymmetry from the energy shock is structurally EUR/AUD negative: Australia's net commodity export position means elevated energy prices improve its terms of trade, while the eurozone's net import dependence worsens its external balance. This divergence will become visible in Q2 trade data, providing a fundamental catalyst. Third, RBA credibility versus ECB uncertainty creates a reliability premium for AUD carry — each RBA hike delivery reinforces the trade while the ECB's conflicted policy stance (stagflation dilemma) introduces uncertainty that penalises EUR. Fourth, the newly concluded EU-Australia FTA creates a medium-term structural flow catalyst: the potential 87% increase in EU investment flows into Australia creates structural EUR-selling/AUD-buying demand over the implementation period. The German fiscal expansion is the primary counter-argument — it could support EUR through growth and fiscal channels — but the spending pipeline is 6-12 months from materialisation and is currently being overwhelmed by the energy shock. The positioning dynamics strategically favour EUR/AUD downside: EUR longs (narrative-vulnerable, 95th percentile) are more likely to unwind than AUD longs (fundamentally anchored), and any AUD spec washout into net short territory creates a powerful contrarian launchpad. The consensus median of approximately 1.72 by late 2026 appears too high relative to the bilateral differential trajectory; our target of 1.58-1.63 reflects the full carry accumulation plus rate divergence impact and aligns with the technical picture where the 61.8% Fibonacci retracement from the 2022-2025 rally sits at 1.5913.

catalysts.rationale: A hot print (2.5-3.0%+) reinforces the eurozone stagflation narrative — inflation rising into falling growth is EUR-negative. It validates the ECB hawkish pivot but the growth damage dominates for EUR valuation, creating a toxic combination. National flash data from Germany (2.8%) and Spain (3.3%) already signal an above-consensus headline. A stagflation print widens the asymmetry between Australia (higher inflation = more RBA hiking = AUD positive) and the eurozone (higher inflation = growth damage = EUR negative), reinforcing the bearish EUR/AUD thesis.

catalysts.event: Trump's Iran Strike Pause Expires — Binary Geopolitical Event
catalysts.rationale: Escalation drives AUD down more than EUR (higher AUD risk beta ~0.3-0.4), pushing EUR/AUD higher toward 1.72-1.74 within 48 hours. De-escalation drives AUD recovery more than EUR on risk-on dynamics, pushing EUR/AUD sharply lower toward 1.62-1.64 within a week as carry flows normalise. The bilateral effect is asymmetric: AUD has higher risk beta than EUR, making this event more impactful for the quote leg. A Hormuz closure or extended escalation would amplify the risk-off channel significantly, overwhelming carry in the short term.

Expected above 4.0% headline / 3.5% trimmed mean, which would lock in the May RBA hike at approximately 70% probability rising to near-certainty. A hot print above 4.5% would force additional tightening expectations beyond May, widening the ECB-RBA differential further and accelerating EUR/AUD downside. This is the most important AUD-specific catalyst in the tactical window after the April 6 deadline. A soft print below 3.0% trimmed mean would reduce May hike probability and could trigger a corrective EUR/AUD bounce toward 1.68-1.70.

No new staff projections make an April hike unlikely — market pricing is for a hold with hawkish language, which is the base case. A hold with hawkish language would be EUR-neutral to mildly negative. A surprise 25bp hike would temporarily boost EUR/AUD toward 1.69-1.70, providing a short re-entry opportunity for bears. The meeting is important as a credibility test for the ECB's hawkish pivot — if Lagarde signals a June hike is data-dependent, the EUR reaction should be muted. For EUR/AUD, this event is a second-order catalyst relative to the April 6 Iran deadline and April 29 Australian CPI.

catalysts.event: RBA Rate Decision + Statement on Monetary Policy (SoMP)
catalysts.rationale: Approximately 70% probability of +25bp hike to 4.35%. The SoMP includes the first post-war forecast revision — likely a significant CPI upgrade given the energy shock. This is the expected inflection catalyst confirming the downtrend continuation and validating the strategic thesis. The combination of a hike delivery plus upgraded inflation forecasts signalling further hikes would push EUR/AUD toward 1.63-1.65, breaking through or testing the 1.6350 technical target. The magnitude of the SoMP forecast revisions is not priced — this is the key information surprise potential of this event.

Defence spending uplift and fuel excise continuation are expected. A fiscally stimulatory budget supports Australian growth (AUD-positive through growth channel) but complicates the RBA's inflation task (mildly AUD-negative through rates channel as it may require additional hikes). The net AUD impact depends on market interpretation — if growth-supportive, AUD strengthens and EUR/AUD falls; if inflationary, the RBA hiking path extends, also AUD-positive. The bilateral transmission is broadly EUR/AUD negative regardless of the specific budget composition, as Australia's stronger fiscal position relative to eurozone peripheral constraints reinforces the sovereign quality differential.

Key strategic ECB meeting with updated staff projections — the first full assessment of the post-war economic landscape. If projections show persistent inflation with moderate growth, a rate hike is likely — EUR-positive (mildly EUR/AUD positive). If the GDP forecast is downgraded sharply (sub-0.5% 2026) while inflation remains elevated, the ECB faces a stagflation dilemma and may hold — EUR-negative (EUR/AUD bearish). The bilateral relevance is high because the June ECB decision will set the trajectory for H2 2026 ECB policy, determining whether the ECB-RBA differential widens to -235bps (base case) or narrows to -185bps (ECB hawkish scenario). The parallel RBA June meeting creates a double catalyst for EUR/AUD.

A second consecutive hike from the May meeting would bring the cash rate to 4.60%, representing the widest positive ECB-RBA spread in modern history at approximately -260bps (vs ECB at 2.00%) or -235bps (vs ECB at 2.25% after June hike). This would be a maximum carry catalyst for short EUR/AUD positions, accelerating institutional reallocation from EUR-denominated to AUD-denominated assets. The combination of a June RBA hike with the ECB's June projections — occurring just days apart — creates a compounding bilateral catalyst that could push EUR/AUD through 1.60 toward the 1.5913 Fibonacci target.

Could deliver the cash rate to 4.85% — the projected terminal rate per Westpac forecasts. A terminal rate catalyst represents the maximum carry differential for EUR/AUD and would validate the full strategic thesis. The August SoMP would confirm whether the RBA intends to pause after reaching terminal or continue — a hawkish hold signal would still be EUR/AUD negative through the carry maintenance channel. If achieved, the -285bps ECB-RBA differential (assuming ECB at 2.00%) represents the strongest carry incentive in G10 FX for short EUR positions and would attract systematic carry fund allocations.

invalidators.condition: EUR/AUD closes above 1.7245 for 3 consecutive daily closes
invalidators.mechanism: A sustained close above 1.7245 signals the medium-term downtrend from 1.8554 has reversed and the 1.8554-1.6130 decline is being structurally retraced. This level represents the support-turned-resistance of the H1 2026 downtrend and its breach invalidates the descending channel structure and the technical bearish case built on lower highs and lower lows.

invalidators.condition: RBA pauses its hiking cycle at the May or June meeting when a hike was 60%+ priced by OIS markets
invalidators.mechanism: An RBA pause against market expectations would undermine the primary rate differential thesis and signal deterioration in Australian growth or labour market conditions not yet priced. It would narrow the ECB-RBA differential trajectory from the projected -285bps to potentially -210bps or less, reducing carry attraction and potentially triggering AUD long unwinding from crowded 95th percentile positions.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: RBA policy decision and statement; OIS-implied probability

invalidators.condition: Brent crude sustains below $80/bbl for 10+ consecutive trading days
invalidators.mechanism: Sub-$80 oil would eliminate the eurozone terms-of-trade deterioration that is the second-most-important EUR/AUD bearish driver. EUR would recover as the current account pressure abates, while AUD would lose some commodity premium (though coal/LNG support would persist). At sub-$80 oil, the carry differential dominates but is less amplified by the external balance channel — the thesis weakens to a simpler (but less compelling) carry trade.

invalidators.condition: BTP-Bund spread narrows below 70bp AND ECB delivers 50bp+ hike in a single meeting
invalidators.new_stance: Bullish EUR/AUD — ECB hawkishness overwhelms RBA differential
invalidators.mechanism: A 50bp+ ECB hike combined with peripheral spread normalisation would signal decisive ECB hawkishness without fiscal constraint, potentially narrowing the ECB-RBA differential by 50bps in a single step while simultaneously signalling further ECB tightening. This combination would represent a fundamental re-rating of EUR relative to AUD and could push EUR/AUD back toward 1.75+.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: BTP-Bund spread (Bloomberg) and ECB rate decisions
invalidators.monitor_frequency: Daily for spread; per meeting for ECB

invalidators.condition: China GDP prints below 4.0% for Q1 or Q2 2026 (NBS release)
invalidators.mechanism: Sub-4% China growth would crush iron ore demand (iron ore ~$106/T currently; could fall toward $75-80/T), undermine Australia's terms-of-trade improvement thesis, and trigger a China-proxy AUD selloff that overwhelms the carry differential. AUD has historically fallen 5-8% on China recession scares, which would partially or fully offset the carry advantage and weaken the external balance EUR/AUD bearish case.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: China NBS GDP quarterly release; iron ore 62% Fe CFR China spot price

invalidators.condition: VIX sustains above 40 for 5+ consecutive sessions
invalidators.new_stance: Conviction reduced to 2/5 — crisis-level risk aversion overwhelms carry
invalidators.mechanism: Full crisis-level risk aversion at VIX 40+ would overwhelm carry and rate differentials through AUD's high-beta channel, temporarily reversing the EUR/AUD downtrend as AUD sells off disproportionately. Historical precedents (COVID 2020, GFC 2008) show AUD/EUR can spike 15-20% in acute risk-off episodes regardless of carry fundamentals. The thesis remains intact for eventual recovery but tactical holding would become untenable.

signposts.confirms_if: Widens above +220bps, signalling increasing yield-seeking flow incentive from EUR to AUD-denominated fixed income
signposts.invalidates_if: Narrows below +150bps, signalling rate convergence that undermines the carry and flow thesis
signposts.bilateral_importance: This is the single most important real-money flow driver for EUR/AUD — widening spread attracts institutional reallocation from European to Australian bonds. Japanese institutions (¥213.6bn AGBs purchased June 2025) and European pension funds respond directly to this spread, creating persistent structural AUD demand. No other signpost has a more direct mechanical link to EUR/AUD directional flows.

signposts.confirms_if: Falls below $90/bbl, reducing EUR energy import cost burden while AUD commodity support from coal/LNG persists — carry differential dominates and EUR/AUD falls
signposts.invalidates_if: Rises above $130/bbl and sustains for 5+ sessions, triggering AUD risk-off selling that overwhelms carry
signposts.bilateral_importance: Energy prices create the asymmetric external balance impact that is EUR/AUD's second-most-important driver. The eurozone's 58% energy import dependence means each $10/bbl increase deteriorates its current account by approximately 0.3pp GDP, while Australia's net energy export position means the same move generates +A$3-7B annual trade benefit. The oil price is the primary determinant of whether the terms-of-trade channel amplifies or dampens the rate differential thesis.

signposts.confirms_if: Falls below 22, enabling carry reassertion and risk normalisation; AUD's high-beta means it outperforms EUR on recovery, pushing EUR/AUD lower
signposts.invalidates_if: Sustains above 35 for 5+ sessions, signalling crisis-level risk aversion that overwhelms carry through AUD's high-beta channel
signposts.bilateral_importance: VIX directly determines the carry-risk trade-off for EUR/AUD. Below 22, the -210bps carry differential dominates and EUR/AUD falls. Between 22-35, carry competes with risk-off dynamics. Above 35, risk dominates and EUR/AUD rises because AUD's higher risk beta (-0.3-0.4 correlation) means it underperforms EUR in acute stress. The VIX level is therefore the gating condition for whether the bearish EUR/AUD thesis is in active vs suppressed mode.

signposts.confirms_if: Terminal repriced above 5.0%, widening the ECB-RBA differential beyond -285bps and accelerating carry flow into AUD
signposts.invalidates_if: Terminal repriced below 4.35%, implying RBA is pausing and the primary rate differential thesis is undermined
signposts.bilateral_importance: The RBA terminal rate directly determines the maximum ECB-RBA spread — the primary fundamental driver of EUR/AUD. Each 25bp increment in the RBA terminal rate adds approximately 80-100 pips of EUR/AUD downside potential through carry accumulation and real-money flow reallocation. The OIS curve is the most timely forward-looking signal for this driver.

signposts.confirms_if: Sustains above $110/T toward $120, validating China demand and supporting Australia's terms-of-trade improvement thesis
signposts.invalidates_if: Falls below $90/T for 5+ sessions, signalling China demand deterioration that would undermine AUD's commodity channel
signposts.bilateral_importance: Iron ore is Australia's largest single export (~A$120B annually) and its price is a direct barometer of AUD's commodity-driven external balance strength. Sustained weakness below $90 would undermine the external balance improvement thesis that supports AUD over EUR, particularly if driven by China growth disappointment. The iron ore-AUD correlation is approximately 0.6-0.7 on a 3-month basis, making it the most important individual commodity signpost for the EUR/AUD thesis.

signposts.confirms_if: Spot remains below 1.6924 (downtrend intact); 100d SMA continues declining
signposts.invalidates_if: Spot closes above 1.6924 for 3+ consecutive sessions, signalling the corrective bounce is becoming a structural reversal
signposts.bilateral_importance: The 100-day SMA at 1.6924 is the first significant technical resistance in the bearish structure — a reclaim would signal the corrective bounce from 1.6130 is becoming a full reversal rather than a retracement. Technical traders and systematic funds use this level as a trend filter; a sustained break above would trigger covering of short EUR/AUD positions and potentially activate trend-following systems on the long side, amplifying the move. This level is also meaningful because it sits approximately 190 pips above the current spot, providing a clear quantitative threshold for monitoring.

assumptions: Carry calculations use the ECB deposit rate (2.00%) versus RBA cash rate (4.10%) as the bilateral differential proxy. Actual forward points may differ due to cross-currency basis and liquidity conditions. Exact EUR/AUD forward points are estimated from interest rate parity, not directly observed market quotes.

PPP fair value estimates for EUR/AUD are triangulated from both dossiers' PPP tables: EUR/AUD PPP implied at approximately 1.58-1.61. These are OECD-based estimates with 12-18 month data lag and should be treated as broad equilibrium anchors rather than tactical price targets.

Options data (implied volatility, risk reversals, vol surface) for EUR/AUD specifically was not available from public sources. Estimates of 11.5-12.5% 1M IV are derived from the underlying EUR/USD and AUD/USD options data reported in each dossier — the actual EUR/AUD vol may differ due to correlation effects.

The EU-Australia FTA conclusion on March 24, 2026 is a newly identified bilateral catalyst not present in either dossier. Implementation timeline and flow magnitude are estimated based on EU Commission projections; actual flows will depend on ratification, which may take 12-24 months.

CFTC positioning data is reported for EUR and AUD individually against USD. The pair-implied positioning is inferred from the combination but is not directly observable — the directional signal relies on the narrative quality assessment (EUR longs narrative-vulnerable vs AUD longs fundamentally anchored). Direct EUR/AUD positioning data is not available.

Weight adjustments boost Factor 1 (Policy & Rates) by +0.03 at both horizons reflecting the extreme bilateral rate differential (200bps) which is the dominant pair driver. Factors 3 (Risk Appetite) and 10 (USD Liquidity) are marginally reduced because EUR/AUD is a cross pair with moderate risk beta and no direct USD leg. Residual weight from Factor 3 (-0.02 tactical, -0.01 strategic) and Factor 10 (-0.01 tactical, -0.02 strategic) is redistributed to Factor 1.

Technical support and resistance levels are sourced from web research (ActionForex, TradingView, CoinCodex) as these require visual chart analysis beyond what OHLC data provides. Fibonacci retracement levels (1.5913, 1.6351) are computed from the 2022 low (1.4281) and the 2025 high (1.8554/1.8556).

The valuation date is March 31, 2026. Spot reference of 1.6736 is the latest close as of April 1, 2026. There is a one-day lag between the stated valuation date and the spot reference, reflecting the analysis compilation timeline.

Iran-related geopolitical probabilities (escalation, resolution) are qualitative assessments based on available public information as of the analysis date and carry significant uncertainty. These are not derived from prediction market data.

The strategic scenario probabilities (base case 50%, upside 20%, downside 30%) reflect an asymmetric distribution favouring the downside scenario over the upside given the strength of structural bearish factors. Tactical scenario probabilities are split 50/25/25 reflecting greater near-term binary uncertainty from the April 6 Iran deadline.
